risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were randomly assigned to the intervention or usual care control group in a 1:1 ratio. Random treatment assignments were placed in numbered opaque envelopes." Comment: The study explicitly states that participants were randomly assigned, and the use of numbered opaque envelopes is consistent with a proper random allocation process; although the exact random-number method is not named, the description provides sufficient context to judge low risk for sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: Subjects were randomly assigned to the intervention or usual care control group in a 1:1 ratio. Random treatment assignments were placed in numbered opaque envelopes.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomly assigned 1:1 to intervention or usual care using numbered opaque envelopes.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To protect the blind, the research assistant collected baseline data after obtaining informed consent, but before randomization. Random treatment assignments were placed in numbered opaque envelopes." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism using numbered opaque envelopes, which prevents the recruiter from foreseeing the upcoming assignment before enrollment/randomization. Although the text does not separately say "sealed," the described numbered opaque envelope procedure is the stated allocation concealment method and there are no signals of predictability.

_evidence.concealment_quote: To protect the blind, the research assistant collected baseline data after obtaining informed consent, but before randomization. Random treatment assignments were placed in numbered opaque envelopes.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "In this study, we conducted an 8-week, 2-arm, parallel, single-blind, pilot trial in which candidates for treatment with an initial or new biologic were randomized to receive a link to the web-based tool or to usual care." Comment: This was only single-blind, and the intervention was a web-based decision aid versus usual care, so participants were not blinded and personnel delivering the intervention were aware of assignment. Because key outcomes included subjective knowledge, value clarity, risk communication, informed choice, and satisfaction with communication, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes; therefore the dominant risk for this domain is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote:
In this study, we conducted an 8-week, 2-arm, parallel, single-blind, pilot trial in which candidates for treatment with an initial or new biologic were randomized to receive a link to the web-based tool or to usual care.

To protect the blind, the research assistant collected baseline data after obtaining informed consent, but before randomization.

Follow-up data were collected over the telephone by trained blinded interviewers using a standardized script at two and eight weeks. Subjects were reminded at the beginning of each follow-up call not to discuss any prior study-related procedures.
_evidence.blinding_method: single-blind; trained blinded interviewers; participants were randomized to receive a link to the web-based tool or to usual care; no attention control arm

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Follow-up data were collected over the telephone by trained blinded interviewers using a standardized script at two and eight weeks. ... Chart review was performed without knowledge of group assignment." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded for the patient-reported follow-up measures, and chart review for the objective outcomes was also performed without knowledge of assignment. The study reports a mix of patient-reported and objective outcomes, but the subjective outcomes were assessed by blinded interviewers, so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Follow-up data were collected over the telephone by trained blinded interviewers using a standardized script at two and eight weeks. ... Chart review was performed without knowledge of group assignment.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Follow-up data were not available for three subjects randomized to the intervention arm and for five subjects randomized to the control arm. 117 (94%) subjects were contacted for both follow-up surveys." Comment: The analysed outcome data appear largely complete and balanced across arms (3/60 vs 5/61 missing; about 6.4% total loss, 1.6 percentage-point difference), with no evidence of outcome-related differential attrition or problematic exclusion. This is below the usual thresholds for attrition bias and supports low risk.

per_arm_flow.reasons: Follow-up data were not available for three subjects randomized to the intervention arm and for five subjects randomized to the control arm. 117 (94%) subjects were contacted for both follow-up surveys.

Follow-up data were not available for three subjects randomized to the intervention arm and for five subjects randomized to the control arm. Fifteen subjects (24%) did not access the tool after five days and were called. Of these, three subjects did not access the tool and were analyzed as part of the intervention group.

_evidence.notes: Attrition appears to refer to follow-up data availability at 2 and 8 weeks; per-arm analysed numbers are taken from Table 1 (of sample with 2 week data) and text indicating 61 control and 60 intervention were randomized, with 3 intervention and 5 control lacking follow-up data.
